flashcards.category: AP Physics C: Mechanics - Systems of Particles & Momentum

cards.front: Define **center of mass** for a system of particles.
cards.back: $\vec{r}_{\text{cm}} = \frac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$

cards.front: Center of mass for a continuous body?
cards.back: $\vec{r}_{\text{cm}} = \frac{1}{M} \int \vec{r}\,dm$

cards.front: Velocity of the center of mass?
cards.back: $\vec{v}_{\text{cm}} = \frac{\sum m_i \vec{v}_i}{M} = \frac{\vec{p}_{\text{total}}}{M}$

cards.front: Acceleration of the center of mass?
cards.back: $\vec{a}_{\text{cm}} = \frac{\Sigma \vec{F}_{\text{ext}}}{M}$

cards.front: Does Newton's 2nd Law hold for the center of mass?
cards.back: Yes: $\Sigma \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = M\vec{a}_{\text{cm}}$
$\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$
$\vec{P} = \sum \vec{p}_i = M\vec{v}_{\text{cm}}$
$\vec{J} = \int_{t_i}^{t_f} \vec{F}\,dt = \Delta \vec{p}$
$\vec{F}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\vec{J}}{\Delta t} = \frac{\Delta \vec{p}}{\Delta t}$
If $\Sigma \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = 0$, then $\vec{P}_i = \vec{P}_f$.
When the net external force on the system is zero.
A system with no net external force acting on it.
Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.
Momentum conserved; kinetic energy is NOT conserved.
Momentum conserved; objects stick together after collision. Maximum KE loss.

cards.front: Is KE ever conserved in a perfectly inelastic collision?
cards.back: No. Some KE is always converted to thermal/internal energy.

cards.front: 1D elastic collision: equal masses, one at rest. Result?
cards.back: Moving mass stops; stationary mass moves with initial velocity. Full momentum and energy transfer.

cards.front: 1D elastic collision: $m_1 \ll m_2$, $m_2$ at rest. Result?
cards.back: $m_1$ bounces back with roughly same speed; $m_2$ barely moves.

cards.front: 1D elastic collision: $m_1 \gg m_2$, $m_2$ at rest. Result?
cards.back: $m_1$ continues with nearly unchanged velocity; $m_2$ shoots forward at roughly $2v_{1i}$.

cards.front: Energy lost in a perfectly inelastic collision?

cards.back: Conserve momentum independently in $x$ and $y$ directions: $P_{x,i} = P_{x,f}$, $P_{y,i} = P_{y,f}$.

cards.front: What is an **explosion** in momentum terms?
cards.back: Reverse of perfectly inelastic collision. $P_i = 0$, $P_f = 0$. Fragments fly apart such that $\sum m_i \vec{v}_i = 0$.
Thrust $= -u\frac{dm}{dt}$, where $u$ is exhaust speed (magnitude, negative sign from dm/dt < 0).
$v_f - v_i = u\ln\left(\frac{m_i}{m_f}\right)$

cards.front: In the rocket equation, what is $u$?
cards.back: Exhaust speed relative to the rocket.

cards.front: Why is momentum conserved but KE not in inelastic collisions?
cards.back: Momentum is always conserved (no external forces). KE transforms to thermal/sound/deformation energy.
A device measuring projectile speed via perfectly inelastic collision with a pendulum bob.
Momentum during collision: $mv = (m+M)V_f$. Then energy: $\frac{1}{2}(m+M)V_f^2 = (m+M)gh$. Solve for $v$.
During a brief collision, external forces are negligible compared to large impulsive forces. $\Delta p \approx 0$ for the system.

cards.front: Force-time graph: what does the area represent?
cards.back: Impulse $J = \int F\,dt$.

cards.front: Two particles approach CM; what is $\vec{v}_{\text{cm}}$?
cards.back: Constant if no external forces. CM moves uniformly.

cards.front: A system's CM can accelerate only if?
cards.back: There is a net external force.
Work-energy: force over **distance** changes KE. Impulse-momentum: force over **time** changes momentum.

cards.front: What is the SI unit of momentum?
cards.back: $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$

cards.front: What is the SI unit of impulse?
cards.back: $\text{N}\cdot\text{s}$, equivalent to $\text{kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$.

cards.front: Two objects collide in 2D elastically. How many equations?
cards.back: 3: conservation of $p_x$, $p_y$, and kinetic energy.
